new_findings.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
new_findings.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made
Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

new_findings.topic: Inbound creator acquisition via personal brand
new_findings.title: Build personal brand (YouTube + IG) so top creators DM you first

variants.claim: High-quality creators with good work ethic come inbound to agencies that have visible authority content (YouTube, IG reels), eliminating the need to chase them.
variants.what_they_do: Oliver posts OFM-focused reels on a dedicated IG account (~10K followers) and maintains a YouTube channel since 2023; creators DM him directly saying they're looking for an agency. He signs them on the first call. He explicitly states creators who DM you first already see you as an authority, while creators you have to chase tend to have poor work ethic.

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

sources.quote: this girl called DM'd me on my OFM Oliver Instagram where I post a bunch of reels
the creators worth taking on…they usually come and find you. You don't try to find them.
your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6 — THIN). No documented inbound content flywheel. This is the opposite motion: competitor earns inbound by investing in public-facing authority content, Palm currently spends effort chasing creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public-facing OFM authority presence for Palm — even a small dedicated IG account posting agency results/tips reels. This converts acquisition from outbound grind to inbound pull, and pre-qualifies creators (they arrive already trusting Palm). Start with one reel/week; track how many creator DMs it generates over 90 days.

new_findings.title: Never pause acquisition — run it as a 24/7 autopilot system

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

variants.claim: Recruiting is the primary scaling lever for an OFM agency because creator quality and quantity directly determine revenue ceiling, yet most agencies treat it as an afterthought rather than a dedicated, always-on department.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs recruiting as a named department with dedicated effort and headcount, not delegated to the CEO's spare time. The principle is that the ideal creator (great look, great work ethic, fully cooperative) effectively makes the agency's systems and marketing effort secondary — and that five such creators could produce $200–300k/month. Recruiting never pauses regardless of current creator count.

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

sources.quote: the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running
Your recruiting shouldn't stop. Your recruiting shouldn't be left on the side. It's a full department in your agency that if you don't take it seriously, you're always going to be stuck.
the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and effectively pauses when attention shifts to delivery (§6). There is no documented funnel, VSL, or call-setting layer, and acquisition is not yet automated or delegated.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's current outreach method into an SOP, then hire/delegate outreach execution so acquisition runs independently of delivery. Add a funnel layer (lead-magnet video → VSL → booked call) to qualify higher-tier creators before the call. Treat acquisition as an always-on background process, not a sequential phase.

new_findings.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out
It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition approach is Josh-DM-led without a documented onboarding capacity limit (§6). There is no written rule about how many creators Palm takes on relative to operational readiness (chatting outsourced §8, thin content vault §2). This principle is implicitly understood but not codified as a scaling gate.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's current operational capacity ceiling per creator tier — e.g., how many creators can be served well given current editor headcount, outsourced chat team bandwidth, and social pipeline throughput. Use this as a hard gate in the acquisition process so new signings only happen when capacity exists, preventing the quality dilution Dylan describes.

new_findings.topic: Minimum viable operating system / weekly scorecard
new_findings.title: Run the business on a 4-part minimum viable operating system anchored by a weekly scorecard

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.
Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has strong content pipeline instrumentation (§1) and invoicing automation (§7), but no weekly business-level scorecard covering all four domains, no delivery operating system for creator accounts, and no documented founder replacement ladder (§10 GAP, §9 GAP). The SMM hub redesign (§11) addresses social content visibility but not the business-health scorecard layer Dylan describes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'Agency Weekly Scorecard' page in the admin portal (or a recurring Airtable view) covering all four scorecard domains. Prioritize delivery tracking first (content compliance + chat performance + revenue per creator) since that's where Palm currently has the most instrumentation gaps. The founder replacement ladder should be applied immediately to Evan and Josh's recurring tasks — document and delegate at least one recurring task per week. This is a direct complement to Palm's in-flight SMM hub consolidation (§11).

new_findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
new_findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

new_findings.topic: Three non-negotiable reel criteria: aesthetics, emotional curve, WTF moment
new_findings.title: Reject any reel missing aesthetics + emotional curve + pattern-interrupt — reshoot it

variants.claim: The difference between 6K-view and 6M-view reels is an engineered three-part formula: matching aesthetics to creator USP, a scripted look-away→look-in→smile emotional arc, and a deliberate stop-scroll pattern-interrupt moment.
variants.what_they_do: Aesthetics: outfit, location, lighting, framing all matched to the creator's visual USP (e.g., if USP is large chest + black hair, 80% of content angles highlight that — not hidden in hoodies). Emotional curve: reels are scripted with a 3-beat arc — look away (living her life) → look in (she noticed you) → smile (girlfriend moment) — engineered to create GFE in 3 seconds, repeated across the ~46 views a typical fan sees before subscribing. WTF moment: every reel has a deliberate background event (someone walking into a pole, a cat crossing) that breaks autopilot scrolling and triggers rewatches, which signals the algorithm.

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: Look away says she's living her life. She's not looking at you. Look in says she just noticed you watching. Then smile.
the emotional curve is the precise difference between a clip that gets 6,000 views and a clip that hits 6 million views
every reel has a stop scroll moment…something in the background, something natural, something the viewer didn't expect
every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies *what* to film from viral data but does not codify *how* to film it at this granularity. The emotional curve and WTF moment criteria are absent from Palm's current documented production standards. Palm's editor workflow has approval gates but no stated rejection criteria at this level.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add these three criteria as a mandatory editor QA checklist in Palm's Task/approval workflow. For aesthetics: add a 'creator USP card' to each creator's Airtable record (visual USP, don'ts list) that editors reference on every clip review. For emotional curve and WTF moment: create a one-page shoot brief template with examples that goes to creators/videographers before every filming session. Clips failing any criterion should be flagged for reshoot, not edited around.

new_findings.topic: AI reel animation — 'secret prompt' technique for virality
new_findings.title: Extract the core action from a viral reel as the animation prompt to replicate its hook

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

variants.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
variants.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.

sources.quote: the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible
screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best
see a trend, generate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline includes AI vision analysis of inspo reels (transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score) but the output is a filming idea for a real creator, not an animation prompt for synthetic video. This technique is specifically for AI content production and has no current Palm equivalent. The underlying logic — isolate the hook action, not just the aesthetic — is relevant to Palm's brief-writing for real creators too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona build-toward (§3/§0): incorporate 'action extraction prompt' as a named step in the synthetic reel SOP — after screenshotting inspo, explicitly ask GPT 'what is the specific physical action happening in this reel?' and use that answer as the primary animation prompt. For real creators: consider adding 'primary hook action' as a structured field in the inspo analysis output (§1, step 3) to sharpen filming briefs.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Use an 'innocent yes-ladder' question to psychologically obligate the fan before sending a PPV

variants.claim: Asking 'Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy — do you promise to go easy on me?' gets a yes-commitment that makes the fan feel obliged to purchase when the PPV arrives.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter asks a softly framed question that (a) gets a 'yes' and (b) maintains the model's innocent persona. The two components are explicit: 'can I film for you' = the commitment; 'I'm a bit shy' = innocence framing. Once the fan agrees, the PPV is sent. The felt obligation drives conversion.

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

sources.quote: Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy? Do you promise you will go easy on me?
he feels obliged that he has to buy it because he already said yes to filming the video
make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is a specific pre-sale conversion mechanic not present in any Palm SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate a scripted yes-ladder template into the in-house chatter playbook as the mandatory bridge between rapport and first PPV send. A/B test the exact phrasing ('can I film for you' vs. 'I made something for you') and track conversion rate per chatter.

new_findings.title: Three-stage follow-up protocol for unopened/unpurchased PPVs — guilt-trip only as last resort

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't buy, a structured follow-up sequence (curiosity ping → reason discovery → resolution → guilt-trip last resort) recovers sales without burning the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — curiosity ping if left on seen: 'Where did you go?' / 'Don't just stare at it, you're making me nervous.' Stage 2 — if he replies but won't buy, ask why (trust issue, no money, bad timing) and resolve the specific objection (offer AI verification tip, suggest waiting). Stage 3 — only if he refuses to explain and is likely a time-waster: guilt-trip ('you made me film it and I'm not getting anything').

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: If he left you on scene, you want to follow up, but don't be negative yet. Don't guilt trip. Use that as the last resort.
you want to find out why he isn't buying and resolve it

if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm has no documented follow-up or objection-handling flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this three-stage ladder into the in-house chatter SOP with explicit scripts for each stage. Flag time-wasters in the Fan Tracker (§9) after Stage 3 failure so chatters deprioritize them per the subscriber-priority queue (see finding on emoji tagging below).

new_findings.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
new_findings.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated
you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

new_findings.topic: Monthly NPS survey system with action protocol
new_findings.title: Send a monthly NPS form to every active client and require a response to every score

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

sources.quote: NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore
set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9) for fan retention, but no documented NPS or satisfaction survey system for creator retention. The §9 gap notes no written retention/churn/win-back strategy. This tactic maps directly to that gap — applied to Palm's creators rather than brand clients.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a monthly creator satisfaction pulse (can be a simple Airtable form or Typeform): 1–10 score, one open question ('what could we do better?'), and one win-capture question ('any recent milestone we should celebrate?'). Route responses to Airtable, auto-alert Josh on any score below 7, and have a 48hr response SOP. This costs near-zero and directly addresses the §9 retention gap.

new_findings.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can
you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

new_findings.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
new_findings.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message
For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

new_findings.topic: Reddit asset spoofing to prevent cross-account detection
new_findings.title: Spoof every reused image via @spoofsybot; use RedGIFs re-upload to spoof videos

variants.claim: Reusing un-spoofed images or videos across Reddit accounts links them and triggers chain bans; spoofing tools strip the metadata/hash that Reddit uses to connect accounts.
variants.what_they_do: For images: run all reused assets through @spoofsybot on Telegram (paid, cheap, offers free trial) before posting. For videos/GIFs: upload fresh to RedGIFs each time — RedGIFs auto-generates a new file fingerprint, functionally spoofing the video. Never repost raw files across accounts.

variants.claim: Tinder (and similar apps) use Amazon Rekognition to create a text-based fingerprint of every uploaded photo, so metadata removal and even pixel-level cloaking software will not prevent re-used images from getting the account shadow-banned or banned.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains strict separation of images used on dating apps from all other platforms. Once an image has been uploaded to Tinder/Bumble it is permanently marked (T code) and never reused there. Fresh, previously undeployed photos are sourced for each new dating-app account cycle. The tracking system described above makes this operationally feasible.

sources.quote: spoofsybot on Telegram… you can spoof images there and you reuse content. Make sure you always do if you want to ever reuse content, you have to always spoof it
Red GFs technically does it for you because all you have to do in red gifs is just upload the video again and use a different link
if you try to reuse images whether you have removed the metadata or not on dating apps the app Flags the account and it will either get Shadow ban or band
they lease Amazon recognition software which ultimately lists every photo uploaded to the database as a text-based fingerprint

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-persona account isolation system (§3 — hardware-isolated, separate SIMs, GrapheneOS) but no documented asset-spoofing workflow for Reddit or any platform. This is a direct gap when scaling multi-account Reddit traffic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add asset-spoofing as a mandatory step in any Reddit posting SOP: integrate @spoofsybot into the content-prep checklist for images and enforce RedGIFs-only video uploads. Document this in the platform-risk runbook alongside existing device-isolation procedures.

new_findings.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it
use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

new_findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right
if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

new_findings.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
new_findings.title: Segment subscriber list into 5+ tiers; send different mass messages per tier daily

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody
We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

new_findings.title: Use Per-Platform Tracking Links to Attribute OF Revenue to Traffic Sources

variants.claim: Operators run the funnel as a tracked numbers game: per-platform tracking links reveal where subs and revenue actually come from and surface conversion bottlenecks.
variants.what_they_do: A separate Fanvue tracking link per social account measures clicks, subs, revenue and per-source CTR; a low CTR (under 1-2%) flags a broken bio/CTA, X funnel or Fanvue setup to fix; spend-per-sub is calculated per source to decide where to invest time and money.

variants.claim: Using a dedicated tracking link for each traffic source (e.g., one link for Twitter) lets you measure exactly how much OF revenue each platform generates.
variants.what_they_do: Each traffic channel (X account, IG, promo page, etc.) gets its own tracking/inflow link. Griffin shows a single Twitter tracking link that generated $186K in OF revenue, proving channel-level attribution. This enables decisions like 'Twitter is responsible for ~40–50% of this creator's revenue growth' rather than guessing.

sources.quote: a direct tracking link created on fan view for X only
A good CTR is 3 to 5%. Less than 1 to 2% means your funnel's weak
This is a tracking link in inflow from one of our Twitter accounts on our creator using this strategy and we've generated $186,000 through this link.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks invoicing/data (§7, §9) and has Fan Tracker, but cross-channel sub attribution and funnel-leak diagnostics are not documented; traffic measurement is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement per-source tracking links plus a per-channel CTR / spend-per-sub view for real creators to attribute OF subs to their social source and diagnose funnel leaks.

new_findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

variants.claim: Embedding hidden checkpoints (emoji signals, mid-video questions) inside a mandatory 90-minute onboarding video filters out candidates who won't follow through — before they ever touch a live fan account.
variants.what_they_do: After passing all screening stages, selected candidates receive a 90+ minute video covering: how OnlyFans works, how the CRM (Creator Hero) works, scripts, hotkeys, and job structure. The video includes 2–3 embedded accountability checks — e.g., 'send me a cherry emoji if you watched to here' or answer specific questions after viewing. Anyone who fails to complete or respond is cut. Rationale: if a chatter won't watch a Loom on Day 1, they won't perform reliably in month 2.

sources.quote: Loom recordings
send them like a 90-minute plus video explaining the entire job... include a couple of accountability checks
if someone isn't even watching a Loom video you sent them at their first day on the job, you can imagine how he's going to work in a month from now

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

new_findings.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

new_findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI
Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

new_findings.title: Treat every revenue-generating asset as likely to disappear tomorrow; diversify across creators, accounts, and traffic sources

variants.claim: Because Instagram accounts, OF accounts, and individual creators can all disappear overnight without warning, any agency with revenue concentrated in a single asset is one ban or personal decision away from zero.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly flags concentration risk as a daily operational concern: monitors the revenue share of each creator, each Instagram account, and each employee. If one creator generates the majority of revenue, the agency treats that as a red flag and accelerates recruiting to dilute the dependency. Same logic applied to Instagram accounts (build more traffic sources, have a ban-response protocol) and key employees (have them document and train backups before they leave).

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: You have one creator that makes the majority of the revenue, that should be a fucking red flag. That should scare the hell out of you.
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona infrastructure already provides meaningful diversification (multiple accounts, hardware-isolated, §3/§4 — MATURE). However, on the real-creator side, creator concentration risk is not formally tracked (§9 — PARTIAL; §7 — GAP). There is no documented ban-response protocol or creator-departure contingency plan in the codebase.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'revenue concentration' metric to the agency health dashboard: flag any single creator, IG account, or traffic channel that exceeds 40% of total revenue. For the top-concentration creator, document a written contingency plan (which backup account takes traffic, who covers DMs, how fast can recruiting replace volume). For IG accounts, formalise the ban-response protocol currently referenced but not codified.

new_findings.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

variants.claim: Reposting low-performing content at scale wastes operational overhead with negligible subscriber return and delays the discovery of a working content formula.
variants.what_they_do: They establish a minimum quality floor first — in their case, videos that reliably hit 50K+ views each — before launching any repurposing infrastructure. Until that floor is hit, all effort goes into improving content quality (hooks, formats, etc.). Only after consistent organic performance do they add repurposing accounts and VA infrastructure.

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.
before you go into the volume, you have to go into the quality… optimize the content until you have a winning formula

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

new_findings.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.
he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses a commission/revenue-split model set per creator at onboarding (§7) — no documented salary-creator option. Palm's split rationale is not written down (§7 GAP). The salary model is a materially different deal structure Palm has not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'salary creator' tier to Palm's deal structure documentation (§7). Define eligibility criteria (e.g. creator has no existing following; Palm does all marketing) and salary bands by geography. This model makes most sense for Palm's AI personas or for raw-talent real creators with no existing audience, where Palm controls 100% of the funnel.

new_findings.title: Set explicit month-1/2/3 milestones with creator at onboarding

variants.claim: Managing a creator's expectations up front — covering communication channels, response SLAs, and realistic revenue ramps per month — is the single most important onboarding step.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines and communicates: which platform to message on, guaranteed response windows, and what revenue/subscriber outcomes are realistic in months 1, 2, and 3. They crowdsource realistic benchmarks from a community of 1,000+ operators to give creators honest projections rather than over-promising.

variants.claim: Proactively recognizing creator milestones (first viral video, top 1%, highest-earning month) via a dedicated relationship manager — not the CEO — is a primary driver of creator retention and prevents the agency-creator 'butting heads' dynamic.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe a specific failure mode: the agency founder misses milestone moments (e.g., creator hits first six figures) because they're buried in ops. The fix is a model-facing manager whose explicit job includes celebrating wins, providing comfortability, and making the creator feel involved in the agency. Examples given: 'you just hit the top 1%,' 'your highest month ever,' 'first 1M video.' This person also handles weekly or daily content feedback, direction relays, and expectation-setting — keeping creators informed and emotionally invested in the partnership.

sources.quote: managing expectations so when I say managing expectations that includes when can she message you on what platform what can she expect in the first week in the first month the second month and the third month

highlighting those Milestones is big for sure like hey let's say you have a new Creator right you tell her hey we just hit the top 1% like congrats or like let's say her she just hit her highest month ever

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented expectations-setting SOP or milestone framework at creator onboarding (§6, §10). There is no written-down month-by-month ramp guide. This is a gap — creator churn risk is highest in the first 90 days when results lag expectations.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Month 1 / Month 2 / Month 3' benchmark doc (subscriber count, revenue ranges, content volume) derived from Palm's existing creator data. Deliver it at contract signing and reference it in a kickoff call. Log agreed expectations in the Creators table so Josh can track against actuals.

new_findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run
take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

new_findings.topic: PPV and welcome message split testing — optimize for revenue-per-100 not buy rate
new_findings.title: Split-test PPV price and welcome message on revenue-per-100-subs metric, not open or buy rate

variants.claim: Optimizing for the obvious metric (buy rate, open rate) consistently picks the wrong winner; the correct optimization target is revenue generated per 100 subscribers, which requires testing the full downstream chain.
variants.what_they_do: First PPV: tested $6, $8, $9, $10. Buy rates were 67%, 65%, 59%, 52% respectively. Most agencies would pick $6 (highest buy rate). They picked $8 because per-100-subs revenue: $6×67=$402 vs $8×65=$520 — 30% more revenue. Additionally, second-PPV buy rate was higher at $8 than $6. Welcome message: tested four variants over four weeks. Variant 1 had 85% open rate (highest). Variant 4 had lowest open rate but highest reply rate. They picked variant 4 because reply rate starts a conversation, and conversations generate sales — open rate without reply is worse than not opening at all. The unifying principle: find the metric that is one step closer to actual revenue, not the most visible one.

variants.claim: Knowing your exact PPV purchase rate, chatting ratio, average reply time, Instagram conversion, views-per-sub, and LTV — and A/B testing every script or tactic in 3+ variants — is the operational discipline that separates scaling agencies from stuck ones.
variants.what_they_do: Agency maintains a live spreadsheet where the owner can quote every core metric from memory or within 2 minutes. For any new script or tactic, they run 3 simultaneous variants across chatters, track unlock/purchase rates per variant, identify the winning element (e.g., a different opening angle), and roll that element into all other scripts. Testing is weekly and continuous.

sources.quote: we tested four prices on a paint account with a free trial. $6, 8, 9, 10…we picked eight because of total revenue
the metric that matters is revenue per 100. How much money do 100 new subscribers generate? That's the number to chase.
We picked variant 4 because it had the lowest view rate but the highest reply rate
what's your PPV purchase rate, what's your chatting ratio, your average reply time, your Instagram conversion, how many views do you need to make per sub

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for PPV pricing or welcome message testing at Palm (§7 — GAP on pricing model; §8 — chatting outsourced). This is pure learn-from-others territory. The revenue-per-100-subs framework is a concrete, implementable KPI Palm should adopt for both the current outsourced team and the future in-house build.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish 'revenue per 100 subscribers' as Palm's primary backend performance KPI. For the current outsourced chat team: request this metric in weekly reporting. When building in-house: codify a split-test protocol for first PPV price (test 4 price points, measure over minimum 100 subs) and welcome messages (test 4 variants over 4 weeks). Build both into a standard new-creator launch checklist. Add 'reply rate' as the primary welcome-message metric in Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9), not open rate.

new_findings.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

new_findings.topic: X engagement farming via competitor reply hijacking
new_findings.title: Reply-Hijack Competitor X Posts With Relevant Matching Content to Steal Followers

variants.claim: Replying to competing creators' posts with a relevant matching photo of your model hijacks their engagement and drives profile visits and follows at zero cost.
variants.what_they_do: Identify 10–20 OF creator accounts in the same niche on X. When those accounts post (e.g., bikini beach pic), the burner account replies with a relevant matching photo of the managed creator and a natural caption (e.g., 'What about my bikini at the beach?'). Also retweet competitor posts to appear in front of their audience. Comment on any post going viral in the broader niche to farm free impressions. VA hired to execute this spam engagement loop throughout the day.

variants.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.

sources.quote: Reply to that with relevant things. What about my bikini at the beach? And then attach a photo of you at your model at a beach. What this is going to do is hijack their engagement.
You guys can hire a VA to do this. I have my VAs to do this. Just make sure your spam engagement.
Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no X presence (§5 GAP). Palm does no competitor engagement farming on any platform currently. The VA execution model aligns with Palm's contractor model (Amin for posting) — this would be a new VA task scope.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up X for a creator, hire or assign a VA specifically for X engagement tasks: daily competitor reply loop, retweet rotation, viral-post comment farming. Define a task card type in Airtable for 'X daily engagement' with a checklist. Budget ~1–2 hrs/day per creator for this role.

new_findings.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

variants.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
variants.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator
Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans
20% real content kept people trusting it
