palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) categorises content through the 8-step inspo flow and separates social from OFTV (§2), but there is no documented five-bucket content taxonomy distinguishing OF feed teasers from PPV vault content from Reddit-specific variants from SFW social. The social vs OF vault split is structural but the sub-categories within each are not defined.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt or adapt a formal content taxonomy in Airtable's Assets table. At minimum add a 'Content Category' single-select field with values: SFW-Social, Lingerie-Feed, NSFW-PPV, Reddit-Specific, Kink-Fetish. This enables filtering, chatter-facing views, and ensures editors and chatters never accidentally schedule premium content to the free feed.

consensus_risers.title: Pay micro-influencers flat rate or % to refer creators to the agency

variants.claim: Partnering with micro-influencers (10K–200K followers) as paid lead-getters via a Loom pitch and flat-rate or rev-share referral fee is an efficient bootstrap acquisition channel.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter identifies chatting agencies and micro-influencers with 10K–200K followers. He sends a personalized Loom video explaining the agency's value proposition with no extra work required from the influencer. Payment is either a flat rate per creator referred or a percentage of that creator's ongoing revenue. Once a creator is signed and gets results, she is also converted into a lead-getter who refers her friends, creating a compounding referral loop.

variants.claim: A productized paid-ads-to-VSL-to-call funnel turns creator acquisition from referral/DM luck into a predictable, scalable pipeline.
variants.what_they_do: Runs paid ads (or organic traffic) to a lead magnet, then a 5-minute direct VSL whose two jobs are to book qualified calls and pre-handle objections; a solid funnel + sales team books 20-40 calls/month at a 75-85% show rate and pre-closes 30-40%.

sources.quote: I would reach out to micro influencers between 10,000 and 200,000 followers. I would send them all a loom breakdown of my proposal.
After the exponential results, I would ask her to refer me to any friends that she has and offer her the same proposal as the initial lead getter.
how we built a funnel that books qualified creator calls on autopilot and pre-closes about 30 to 40% of them
you can quite easily be booking 20 to 40 calls per month, and have a show rate of 75 to 85%

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outreach and referrals (§6) with no systematic referral incentive program or micro-influencer partnership channel documented. No Loom-based pitch process exists. This is a direct gap (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a formal lead-getter program: (1) identify 20–30 micro-influencers or OFM adjacent accounts; (2) create a 3–5 min Loom template covering Palm's value prop; (3) define referral compensation (flat $500–$1,000 per signed creator OR 5–10% of Palm's monthly take from that creator for 6 months); (4) after first successful creator, pitch her as a lead-getter with same terms. Document as an acquisition SOP in §6.

consensus_risers.topic: Organic social as primary agency scaling lever
consensus_risers.title: Scale organic social ops as the #1 agency growth lever in 2026

variants.claim: The largest OFM agencies earning hundreds of millions annually built their scale on excellent organic social content years ago, and the rest of the industry must catch up now.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant frames organic social content production — not chatting infrastructure or paid acquisition — as the defining competency separating top-tier agencies from the rest, calling for agencies to laser-focus on producing excellent creative and scaling their social media operations.

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: 2026 is the year you have to get good at organic social. It is time to laser focus on producing excellent creative and scaling your social media ops
the platforms merely ask for one thing in return. Just stop making dog shit. Create content that people actually want to watch
volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already has a mature, data-driven organic social pipeline (§1) built around viral inspo scraping and an 8-step production workflow. Palm is ahead of most competitors here. However, Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP), which limits the ability to truly 'scale' social ops systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm should codify the real-creator posting cadence (frequency, platform-specific timing) to complete the §1 system. Additionally, document Palm's organic social capability as a formal competitive differentiator for use in creator acquisition pitches (§6). Consider producing case-study evidence of social growth results to use in signing conversations.

consensus_risers.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

consensus_risers.title: Twitter/X is currently the strongest traffic platform; Reddit is high-potential but underutilized

variants.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.

variants.claim: Agencies still spending on Reddit, paid ads, SFS, and GG swaps are watching already-thin margins deteriorate and should reallocate entirely to organic social.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant explicitly names Reddit spend, paid advertising, SFS (shoutout-for-shoutout), and GG (girl-girl) swaps as legacy tactics that are now net-negative when factoring in declining ROI and margin compression, advocating a full pivot to organic social content.

sources.quote: Twitter's the best yeah Twitter okay so Twitter's the best okay um Reddit though would weigh when we start getting that going so I know that's very good for traffic
that's like I would say that's like in my opinion like a super outdated method like it's very like TikTok is completely different people don't understand
Many agencies are still using the old methods, throwing money at Reddit and paid ads and SFS and GG swaps, and their already razor thin margins are getting worse by the day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic strategy across platforms (§5 GAP). The tech spine routes content via Publer and Telegram but there's no prioritization of which platforms drive OF conversions or how to activate Reddit. This finding gives a concrete platform hierarchy: Twitter first (activate and optimize), Reddit second (build out properly), IG ongoing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's traffic platform hierarchy explicitly. Prioritize: (1) Ensure every real creator has an active Twitter/X account with teaser clips being posted — confirm this is happening and measure click-through to OF. (2) Build a Reddit activation SOP: subreddit research, posting cadence, account warming to avoid bans (§platform-risk overlap). (3) Formally retire the copy-paste trending TikTok Telegram-group method as a primary strategy; replace with the data-driven inspo pipeline Palm already has (§1).

consensus_risers.title: Run 3-variant script tests with unlock-rate tracking before standardising any chatting SOP

variants.claim: Running three concurrent script variants with measured unlock rates — rather than just deploying a new script agency-wide — reveals the specific element driving performance and prevents agencies from unknowingly using inferior scripts.
variants.what_they_do: When a new chatting script is introduced, the agency assigns variant A, B, and C to different chatters or conversation batches, tracks PPV unlock rate per variant, diagnoses why the winning variant beat the others (e.g., opening line framing), and only then canonises the best approach across all chatters.

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: test every single one of them. Okay, this script got a 50% unlock rate. The other variation is 30%, this is 55. Okay, why?
If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is currently outsourced at Palm (§8 — OUTSOURCED); no internal script management or test infrastructure exists today. This is a build-toward finding for the planned in-house and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing Palm's in-house chat SOP (§8 roadmap), build variant testing into the process architecture from day one: store script variants in Airtable with a linked 'test result' table capturing unlock rate and revenue per test run. Do not import a competitor's script wholesale — always test 3 variants first.

consensus_risers.title: Generate a formal invoice every time a model payment is made to maintain a legal paper trail

variants.claim: Sending a professional invoice with every model payment creates a paper trail that protects the agency legally and signals operational legitimacy to creators.
variants.what_they_do: Every creator payment triggers an invoice document stored in its own 'Invoices' sub-folder inside the creator's Google Drive directory. The services agreement (contract) is also stored in the same creator folder for easy retrieval.

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

variants.claim: Agencies worried about non-payment should invoice weekly rather than monthly, reducing the maximum amount at risk at any given time.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn mentions weekly invoicing as a practical risk-mitigation option for agencies that are anxious about creators running with earnings. He frames it as a simple operational adjustment rather than a trust-control mechanism, keeping the relationship dynamic healthy while capping downside.

sources.quote: inside will be categorized like this...you'll also have a photo grid invoices because each time we send a payment to the model we create an invoice remember you've got to be professional you've got to leave a paper truck
always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service
if you like the source go join the Discord — I'm going to invoice them weekly

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already generates weekly invoices automatically via Puppeteer PDF + Dropbox + Resend email (§7), so the core behaviour is matched. Palm also stores contracts at onboarding (§6). The incremental difference is that this agency co-locates invoices and contracts inside the creator's shared Google Drive folder, making them accessible to the creator directly — Palm currently delivers invoices by email rather than a shared folder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Minor enhancement only: consider adding a 'Documents' section to the creator's shared folder (or a creator portal page) where they can view all past invoices and their contract on demand, reducing support requests and reinforcing professionalism.

consensus_risers.title: Replicate local folder structure 1-to-1 on Google Drive as business-continuity backup

variants.claim: If the agency's local machine were destroyed, the business should still be able to operate — so every folder and file that is critical to operations is mirrored on Google Drive in the exact same structure.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains a Google Drive that mirrors the local computer: a top-level 'Raw' folder per creator containing sub-folders for raw content, photo grid, invoices, promotional assets, OF stories, and the services agreement (contract). The Raw folder is shared directly with the model (right-click → share access → pink highlight convention so the manager knows it is externally shared) so models can self-upload content.

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: if my laptop dropped or fell in a pool or caught on fire or lightning hit it or a car ran it over could the business still operate if the answer is no then I back everything up to the Google Drive
the Royal folder pink that means that this folder has been shared with the model
the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable + Cloudflare media for asset storage (§1) and Dropbox for invoice delivery (§7). A formal business-continuity / disaster-recovery policy for media assets is not documented. The model self-upload workflow exists (creators upload to become Assets) but the specific shared-folder colour-coding convention and the 'can the business function without this laptop' checklist mindset are not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a formal backup policy: define which data stores are business-critical (Airtable, Cloudflare media, contract PDFs, invoices) and ensure at least one redundant copy exists outside the primary system. For creator onboarding, consider a shared Google Drive or Cloudflare folder (colour/tag-coded as 'creator-accessible') as the upload landing zone, mirroring the model-upload convenience described here.

consensus_risers.topic: Revenue split between subscriptions vs. DMs
consensus_risers.title: Benchmark: most OF revenue comes from messages, not subscriptions

variants.claim: The vast majority of a high-performing creator's OF income comes from DM/PPV revenue, not subscription fees — illustrated by a creator earning $68K from subs vs. $300K+ from messages.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry uses this ratio as a core KPI framing: subscriptions are the door, DM monetization is the business. They orient their entire agency operation (chatters, scripts, PPV strategy) around maximising message revenue rather than subscriber count.

variants.claim: Chatting has been commoditized to revshare deals as low as 5%, so agencies can no longer differentiate on chat quality and must compete on organic social marketing instead.
variants.what_they_do: This consultant advises agencies to deprioritize chatting as a differentiator and instead invest heavily in organic social content production as the primary value proposition to creators — arguing that front-end traffic generation is now the only defensible competitive moat.

sources.quote: only made 68 000 over 300 000 literally from the subscriptions the only fan subscriptions and most of the money as you can see came from the messages themselves
Chatting has now become completely commoditized. Today, just about every agency understands the core principles of good chat and has a fundamentally identical offer
in 2026 and beyond, the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system tracks total creator revenue and commissions (§7) but no documented KPI split between subscription vs. message revenue exists. Palm's content engine (§1) is optimised for top-of-funnel subscriber acquisition; the DM monetisation side is outsourced with limited visibility (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a sub-vs-message revenue split metric to Palm's invoicing dashboard and whale-hunting reports (§9). Use this ratio as a health KPI per creator: if message revenue is <50% of total, the chatting strategy is underperforming. Share this benchmark with the external chat team as an accountability target.

consensus_risers.title: Productize one repeatable service per niche so delivery is copy-paste, not custom each time

variants.claim: Offering a single systematized, repeatable service instead of custom work for every client is what makes an agency scalable and sustainable.
variants.what_they_do: Picks one niche and one core service (e.g., Google Ads lead gen to a landing page). Builds the campaign once, then copies and pastes the structure for every new client. Learns from aggregate campaign data across all clients to improve results over time. Explicitly avoids mixing service types (websites for one client, Google Ads for another, Facebook for a third).

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: once we've decided on the niche, we want to offer productized services, which means a service that we can offer again and again in a very systematized, very automated way
Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is already productized — inspo scrape → analyze → film → edit → post runs the same way for every creator. The OF vault/PPV side (§2) is thin and non-productized. This finding reinforces Palm's existing strength on social and flags that PPV content production needs a similar systematized playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply the same copy-paste productization logic to OF vault/PPV content (§2 gap): define a standard PPV content set structure (e.g., X pieces per week, price tiers, release cadence) that can be templated across all creators rather than decided ad hoc per creator.

consensus_risers.topic: Traffic continuity as model retention lever
consensus_risers.title: Treat consistent traffic delivery as the #1 model retention mechanism

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

variants.claim: If an agency is doing a genuinely good job for a creator, the creator won't withhold payment or leave, because abandoning the agency mid-month costs her more money than she'd gain.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn's argument: the agency's leverage isn't financial control (bank swap) but value delivery. A creator who is earning significantly more with the agency than she would alone won't risk that income by stiffing the agency. He uses this logic to justify switching all clients to invoicing — the quality of results is the retention mechanism.

sources.quote: Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad
if you are that worried then you are doing a shitty job because if you were doing a really good job for the model then you can have confidence that they're not going to go anywhere

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy (§9 🔲 GAP) and no multi-channel traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP). If Palm's real creators are currently IG-dependent for traffic, they face the same churn risk described here. The connection between traffic reliability and model retention is not captured in Palm's current framework.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'traffic continuity during IG downtime' as an explicit SLA in creator agreements and as a tracked KPI in the whale-hunting / fan-tracker dashboards (§9). Build the narrative internally: Palm's job is to deliver traffic regardless of platform conditions. Use this framing in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a differentiator vs. single-channel agencies.

consensus_risers.title: Done beats perfect; minimize record-to-publish friction

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

variants.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
variants.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.

sources.quote: perfection is what kills frequency. So done always beats perfect
I just like to reduce the friction between me having an idea and then making content

say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's editor workflow is heavy and approval-gated (§1: 2 slots/day per creator, revisions) — appropriate for monetizable social assets but slow for founder/brand content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any Palm/founder brand content, run a low-friction lane (bullet points, templated thumbnails, minimal editing) separate from the polished creator-asset pipeline.

consensus_risers.topic: CRM for fan spend tracking to guide PPV pricing
consensus_risers.title: Use a fan CRM (e.g. Inflow) to read spending habits and calibrate per-fan PPV price escalation

variants.claim: A CRM that surfaces each fan's historical spend enables chatters to know exactly how far to push pricing on any individual fan rather than guessing.
variants.what_they_do: The agency uses a CRM tool called 'Inflow' that tracks per-subscriber spending history. Chatters consult this before sending a PPV to decide at what price point to pitch — fans who have spent more get higher asks. The principle is always to move price upward based on observed willingness to pay.

variants.claim: The first action inside a newly-accessed OF account should be sorting existing subscribers by spend to identify whales and avoid wasting chatter time on low-value fans.
variants.what_they_do: Upon first login to the creator's OF account, agency immediately sorts subscribers by total spend, identifying high-value fans to prioritize in DMs and deprioritizing or ignoring low-spend/no-spend subscribers.

sources.quote: if you're using a CRM a customer relationship manager like inflow then those things are going to show you the spending habits for Subs spending habits and then based on these spending habits it shows then you can only go upwards from there
organizing to subscribers seeing who's spent more and who's not organizing them so you don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting tool and Fan Tracker CRM (§9, PARTIAL) that tracks fan data. However, Palm's CRM is primarily a leadership/reporting layer; there is no documented policy for chatters to use fan spend data to calibrate real-time PPV pricing. The external chat team likely does not have structured access to Palm's fan intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bridge the §9 fan intelligence system to the external chat team: share per-fan spend summaries so chatters can price escalate based on data rather than guesswork. For the in-house chat roadmap, build a spend-aware pricing prompt directly into the chatter workflow.

consensus_risers.title: Keep the profile wall teasing, never explicit, to preserve PPV value

variants.claim: Profile/wall content should tease (lingerie, not nudity) so curiosity—and willingness to pay for PPV—stays intact.
variants.what_they_do: Refuses to put nudes or explicit content on the OF wall, reasoning that if the fan sees everything for free or at subscription price there is nothing left to sell; keeps the wall at lingerie-level to sustain curiosity.

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: do not put any nudes... why is the fan going to spend any money when he's basically seen all of that for free
I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social top-of-funnel content (§1 MATURE) but thin on OF-vault/PPV content strategy (§2 THIN); this is a concrete vault-merchandising rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a 'tease-not-reveal on the wall' rule as part of Palm's OF-vault/PPV content guidance.

consensus_risers.title: Match viral inspo accounts to creator's look-type before briefing

variants.claim: Sending creators viral content from models who look like them — same hair color, body type, vibe — dramatically increases the likelihood the creator will replicate it well and that the content will resonate with the right audience.
variants.what_they_do: Agency manually finds a model whose look closely matches the new creator (e.g., 'blonde cheerleader type'), identifies that model's currently-viral videos, and sends them as direct filming references. Creator replicates the format/dance/hook on IG and TikTok.

variants.claim: Copying an Instagram model whose recent reels have a views-to-followers ratio of at least 1:3 (views ≥ 3× followers) ensures you're piggybacking on a genuinely viral template rather than a stagnant one.
variants.what_they_do: Before cloning or face-swapping any source account, operator audits the last 7 reels and checks that total/average views exceed followers by a factor of 3. Accounts with views below follower count are skipped ('negative ratio'). This is the first filter before any content work begins.

sources.quote: try and find her ideas for Content that are similar to her looking so if she's a blond kind of cheerleader type model then fantastic find another model who looks like her and is currently going viral
what you're looking for is identify someone that's actually pumping on Instagram so you can benefit from it… ratio of one to three

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo from a defined list of accounts and uses AI vision to score virality, but the step of matching inspo sources to a specific creator's look-type is not documented. Inspo may be pooled generically across creators rather than look-matched. This is a refinement opportunity inside the existing pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'look profile' field to the Creators table (hair color, body type, vibe tags). When a new creator onboards, seed their Inspo Sources list with accounts that match their look profile rather than only genre/category. The existing scrape + AI-score pipeline then handles the rest.

new_evidence.title: Tag every fan by relationship type and price accordingly

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.
variants.what_they_do: Four fan types (transactional, lover, whale, time-waster) marked with emoji labels in Fanvue fan-notes and placed into platform lists; mass-PPV sent per list excluding whales; same content sold at different prices by tier; whales sold to 1:1 at high anchors, never via mass message.

variants.claim: Adding emoji tags (whale emoji, feet emoji, etc.) directly to a fan's OnlyFans display name lets chatters instantly identify spend tier and kink preference without opening notes, critical when managing high inbox volume.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters edit the fan's name field in the OF inbox to prepend relevant emojis: 🐳 for whale, specific kink emojis (e.g., foot emoji for feet fetish). This means the chatter sees the tag the moment the chat appears in the list and can tailor the opening message without reading back through history.

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

sources.quote: Mark all spenders on relationship types
Target each group with specific mass messages
you can put emojis in their name, right? In their OnlyFans name and you can quickly see what type of subscriber they are
a specific kink they might have if they're into feet. You can put a feet emoji in their name
We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago
if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data collection (§9 PARTIAL) but chatting is outsourced (§8) with no in-house fan-tiering SOP or differential-pricing model; pricing is also a gap (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a fan-tier taxonomy plus differential-PPV pricing ladder into the in-house/AI chatting roadmap, fed from existing Fan Tracker data now; pricing applies to real creators today.

_new_sources.quote: We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

new_evidence.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly

variants.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.

variants.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters skip surface-level questions (age, location) and instead probe for fetishes, hobbies, and occupation. Every paying subscriber gets a notes entry with those details so any chatter picking up the conversation later has context. Non-spenders are deprioritized — no time is wasted building rapport with subs who have not spent money.

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name
jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share
what are they into right maybe they what do they do for work what are their Hobbies obviously if we're talking about only fans specifically what are they into like what are their fetishes
you don't want to be able to report with people who are just going to waste your time
number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer

new_evidence.title: Rapport, transition, then sell

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: Step One is General conversation building report... step two is transitioning to a more sexual conversation
conserving your energy you should never be throwing all of your energy away
in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced; Palm does not own script flow (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward for the in-house and AI chat roadmap. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a mandated rapport-then-transition-then-sell chat framework as the backbone script when chatting comes in-house or is automated.

new_evidence.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.

new_evidence.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

variants.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.

variants.claim: Displaying a price menu in the OF bio immediately cheapens the creator's brand and signals transactionality before any relationship is built.
variants.what_they_do: When taking over a creator account, the first thing this agency removes is the bio price menu (e.g. '$5 photos, $10 packs'). No prices are listed publicly. Pricing is handled exclusively inside DM conversations after rapport is established, which allows for dynamic escalation and avoids anchoring fans to low numbers.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding
the first thing I see when I take over a models only fans account are all these offers something like this there'll be a menu in the bio
as soon as guys subscribe to the only fans account they see this and it feels like an eBay store

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

new_evidence.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
new_evidence.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Motion reels — not static image slideshows or low-movement content — consistently achieve higher reach and should be the exclusive inspo source when building an AI creator's content library.
variants.what_they_do: When scanning Instagram for AI reel inspo, the operator filters specifically for 'motion reels' (active movement, not static poses) in the 100k–1M view range. Static content is excluded from the inspo pool regardless of view count.

always go for motion reels and always aiming for 100 to 1 million views reels

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

new_evidence.title: Offer content bundles to increase average order value over single-item PPV

new_evidence.topic: IG ban survivability via satellite account bench
new_evidence.title: Pre-warm satellite IG accounts as instant replacement bench when main account is banned

variants.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
variants.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway
100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup system (§3/§4) achieves exactly this for synthetic creators — a 90-day warmup produces a ready-to-use account. palm_comparison.recommendation: Maintain a small bench (3–5) of pre-warmed IG accounts per real creator. This directly reduces model churn from traffic loss (a live pain point per §6 — model retention).

new_evidence.title: Score every creator on gross profit, team hours, and operational drag

variants.claim: A creator profitability scorecard — covering direct costs, team hours, founder attention, growth potential, and content consistency — is the core tool that separates good business decisions from emotional guesses.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator Dylan tracks: gross revenue, agency net, direct costs (chat team, editor/VA, software, promo), gross profit, team hours required, founder hours required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag. He illustrates that a creator doing $12k gross with $1k costs and zero management friction (producing $5k profit) can be more valuable than a $20k creator costing $4k in fulfillment (producing $6k profit) but consuming disproportionate attention. variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.

level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour

new_evidence.title: Live-call training with seconds-fast feedback loops and single-point focus

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

variants.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
variants.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.

sources.quote: Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn
having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter training exists (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as the core training protocol for first 2–4 weeks of any new in-house chatter. Build a simple tracker: one 'current focus point' per chatter updated weekly so managers don't multi-task the coaching.

new_evidence.topic: Multi-account setup — preventing Instagram account linkage
new_evidence.title: Differentiate every signal across repurposing accounts to prevent IG cluster-ban

variants.claim: Instagram actively clusters accounts that share profile pictures, sounds, bios, links, and feed content, then bans them as a group.
variants.what_they_do: Repurposing accounts must have: completely different usernames (not sequential variations), different profile pictures, different feed posts, different bios, and different funneling links (Beacons vs Linktree vs direct, etc.). The only common element is the creator's face in video content.

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

variants.claim: Changing only brightness/contrast is insufficient to fool Instagram's duplicate-detection; the video's structure must be genuinely altered.
variants.what_they_do: They download the original Reel watermark-free (SnapTick/SnapInsta), then apply structural edits in any editing app: trim 0.1s from a scene cut, mirror the clip, insert a different opening scene, and also adjust color/contrast slightly — the goal is a video that IS different, not merely looks different.

sources.quote: Different profile pictures, different usernames… different feed posts, different bio, maybe a different funneling setup

if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third
you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.
you're going to take 0.1 second out of one scene. You're going to basically mirror the video, right? You're going to add a different scene at the beginning

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-account warmup system (§3/§4) already enforces hardware and SIM isolation per persona, and uses dedicated aged Gmail accounts. However, Palm's documented warmup is for AI personas, not for multi-account real-creator repurposing. The same differentiation logic should apply if Palm ever runs repurposing account clusters for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm builds a repurposing layer for real creators, extend the existing AI-account differentiation playbook (§3/§4) to cover: username diversity rules, bio templates that vary per account, and link rotation (alternate between Beacons, Linktree, direct OF link). No sequential naming conventions.

new_evidence.topic: Bank-swap as a hard sell — trust dynamics with creators
new_evidence.title: Asking creators to swap bank info is a high-friction pitch; invoicing reduces creator resistance

variants.claim: Requesting a bank-info swap is a 'hard sell' that spooks creators, especially early in the relationship, whereas invoicing leaves the creator in control and removes that friction entirely.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn notes that bank swaps require the creator to actively approve an email verification step, creating a moment of distrust. He frames invoicing as the trust-building alternative: the model never feels she's lost control of her money, and the agency relies on relationship quality (delivering results) rather than financial lock-in to ensure payment.

variants.claim: Agencies can either replace the model's OnlyFans bank details with their own (leaving the model's name intact) or simply invoice the model for their commission percentage after the model withdraws earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Method 1: Agency swaps the bank account number on the creator's OF account to the agency's account, leaving the account name unchanged so OF doesn't flag it; agency receives all revenue, then pays creator their cut. Method 2: Agency leaves bank info as-is, creator withdraws at month/week end, agency sends a formal invoice (date, agreed split, amount owed, payment deadline) and creator pays the agency's cut separately. Bjorn says he now uses Method 2 for nearly all models and is migrating to 100% invoicing.

variants.claim: Swapping OnlyFans bank details to the agency's account works with traditional brick-and-mortar banks even if the account name differs, but most virtual/neobanks (especially Wise) will reject it or ban the account.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn tested Wise for this use case and was banned — Wise requested business verification, then gave him 48 hours to withdraw funds before permanent ban. He found that physical/legacy banks accept name-account mismatches and funds land correctly. He explicitly warns against Wise for this purpose.

sources.quote: It's A Hard Sell so if you haven't been doing this for a while you're on your first model or you haven't really worked with the model for very long this is going to be a hard sale
at the end of the month you send your invoice to the model saying here's everything your account is earned for the month then here's the percentage we graded on minus that
don't use wise for that they're dogs I got banned for doing that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing model (§7) already eliminates this friction. However, Palm has no documented creator-pitch script addressing payment mechanics — this is a §6/§7 gap. Creators may still have anxiety about payment flows even with invoicing if it's not explained upfront.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'how we get paid' explainer to Palm's creator onboarding wizard or pitch deck: 'You keep control of your account and withdraw as normal; we send you a weekly invoice for our agreed commission.' This proactively neutralizes a common creator objection and positions Palm as the trustworthy, professional option vs. agencies that demand bank swaps.

new_evidence.title: Prioritise shareable/controversial content over all other Threads optimisations

variants.claim: Shareability is the single highest-leverage variable on Threads — more important than posting time, hashtags, or any other tactic.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs ongoing trial-and-error testing across content types and, once a shareable/controversial format is identified, they literally copy-paste the same post daily for weeks or months, observing linear compounding growth each time. variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. variants.claim: Strategic comment baiting on Threads boosts engagement signals, but must look human and on-brand to avoid spam detection.
variants.what_they_do: TDM plants 1–10 comment baits per day per account (capped at 25–30 max) — posts or replies designed to provoke comments. They require the bait to be niche-relevant, human-sounding, and directly related to the original post to avoid spam flags. Volume is scaled based on page maturity.

comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page

Build a simple Airtable view or Publer template for repeating top Threads posts without manual re-creation.

new_evidence.title: Identify and solve one operational bottleneck at a time rather than fixing everything at once

variants.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is actively running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign §11, AI chatting project §8, warmup improvements §4, Phase 3 Publer §5). The risk Dylan describes — solving everything at once — is live. Palm does not have a documented prioritization framework for which bottleneck to tackle first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a formal bottleneck log in Airtable or the admin portal: a ranked list of operational constraints with a single 'active focus' flag. At each leadership sync, confirm only one bottleneck is in active resolution. This isn't about slowing down — it's about ensuring each fix is fully deployed and measured before the next one starts. Apply this now to sequence the SMM consolidation, chat buildout, and Phase 3 Publer work.
